Facts.text: The death of Paganism in England: Pagan King Penda of Mercia and his allies are defeated at the Battle of Winwaed by Christian king Oswiu of Bernicia (as told by Bede)
Battle of Morgarten: Swiss beat duke Leopold I of Austria
Rudolph of Oron claims Jews have confessed to poisoning wells
In La Guardia, Spain, 6 Jews & 5 Conversos are accused of ritual murder
Thomas Wolsey is invested as an English Cardinal
Treaty of Schoonhoven: Henry of the Palatinate, Bishop of Utrecht, cedes lands to the Habsburgs in return for security help against resistant citizens
First kosher butcher (Asser Levy) licensed in New Amsterdam (now New York City)
English House of Commons accepts Exclusion Bill
Barrier Treaty, Austria cedes area to Netherlands
NY General assembly permits Jews to omit phrase "upon the faith of a Christian" from abjuration oath
Charles Mason and Jeremiah Dixon begin surveying Mason–Dixon line between Pennsylvania and Maryland
1st Catholic college in US, Georgetown, opens
1st US college magazine, Yale Literary Government, publishes 1st issue
Explorer Zebulon Pike sights Pikes Peak in Colorado
Allied troops occupies Groningen
Tax revolt in Amsterdam
Series of fires kills 10 in Edinburgh, Scotland
Dutch Business Me gets monopoly on opium trade in Java/Madura
Creek-indians lose all their property in US
William Vincent Wallace, conducts the premiere of his first opera, "Maritana" at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane in London, England
1st US poultry show opens in Boston
In Egypt, the Suez Canal, linking the Mediterranean Sea with the Red Sea, is given the necessary royal concession.
1st US mines school opens in basement of Columbia University, NY
Free postal delivery formally inaugurated
Bathe becomes member of Noordduitse Union
American Federation of Labor (AFL) founded (Pittsburgh)
European colonization and trade in Africa are officially regulated at the international Berlin Conference, formalizing the "Scramble for Africa" among European powers
British SS Wah Yeung catches fire on Canton River off Hong Kong
Dom Pedro II, Emperor of Brazil, deposed; republic proclaimed
Dutch soccer club NEC is established in Nijmegen
The Egyptian Museum, the first national museum in the Middle East, opens to the public in its new premises on Ismailia Square (now Tahir Square) in Cairo
Eugen d'Albert's opera "Tiefland" premieres at the Neues Deutsches Theater in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia
Proclamation sets designs for Canadian $5 & $10 gold coins
US Senate 1st invokes the Cloture Rule to end a filibuster, passes Versailles Treaty
Ernst Toller's "Massen und Menschen" premieres in Nuremberg
Free City of Danzig forms under League of Nations protection
League of Nations holds first meeting in Geneva
KYW-AM in Philadelphia PA begins radio transmissions
Dutch Christian Radio Society (NCRV) forms
First formal radio network, RCA takes over AT&T's 25-station network (NBC), including flagship station WEAF in NYC
Walt Disney Art School created
Nobel for chemistry awarded to Harold C Urey (deuterium)
"A Night at the Opera", comedy film directed by Sam Wood, starring the Marx Brothers, is released
Commonwealth of Philippines inaugurated
Nazi-Germany & Japan sign Anti-Komintern pact
First US congressional session in air-conditioned chambers
Farewell Parade of International Brigades in Barcelona
First telecast of an unscheduled event (fire), W2XBT, NY
Anti-German demonstrations in Czechoslovakia
Nazis begin mass murder of Warsaw Jews
The first unemployment compensation law, under the Social Security Act, that of the District of Columbia, was approved for grants by the Social Security Board
1st 75,000 men called to armed forces duty during peacetime
New York City's Midtown tunnel linking boroughs of Manhattan and Queens opens to traffic
Cow Palace opens in San Francisco
World War II: First flight of the Heinkel He 219.
Surprise attack on office of Nethche Bank
Rules revised for election of modern players to the Baseball Hall of Fame
House Committee on Un-American Activities (HUAC) interrogates astronomer Harlow Shapley
Soccer team GVVV forms in Veenendaal
KRON-TV channel 4 in San Francisco, California (NBC) begins broadcasting; call letters a nod to the owners other business, The San Francisco Chronicle newspaper
Arthur Dorrington, 1st black man in organized hockey is signed, by Atlantic City Seagulls of Eastern Amateur Hockey League
NY Yankee Gil McDougald wins AL Rookie of Year
WIBW TV channel 13 in Topeka, KS (CBS/ABC) begins broadcasting
WRBL TV channel 3 in Columbus, GA (CBS) begins broadcasting

1st regularly scheduled commercial flights over North Pole begin as SAS (Scandinavian Airlines) inaugurates DC-6B service between Los Angeles, California and Copenhagen, Denmark, via Greenland and Winnipeg, Manitoba
Poland & Yugoslavia sign trade agreement
US sentences Soviet spy Rudolf Ivanovich Abel to 30 years & $3,000
Cleveland Browns' halfback Bobby Mitchell sets club record for longest run from scrimmage (90-yards), beat Wash 31-17
USS G Washington, 1st sub with nuclear ballistic missiles, launched
Comet C/1961 T1 (Seki) approaches within 0.1019 AUs of Earth
UN bans nuclear arms
Valium, created by chemist Leo Sternbach at Roche, approved by US Drug and Food Administration - most prescribed drug in the world 1969-1982
Ajax soccer star Johan Cruijff debuts against GVAV
KBYU TV channel 11 in Provo, UT (PBS) begins broadcasting
Sudan Premier Ibrahim Abbud resigns

The only fatality of the X-15 program occurs during the 191st flight when Air Force test pilot Michael J. Adams loses control of his aircraft descending from 81km, causing its mid air destruction over the Mojave Desert
WLTV TV channel 23 in Miami, FL (IND) begins broadcasting
1st Jackson Five record to enter top 100 (I Want You Back)
The 1st ever colour television commercial in the UK, for Birds Eye peas, airs on ATV in the Midlands during a Thunderbirds episode
Wendy's Hamburgers, American fast food restaurant chains founded by Dave Thomas opens in Columbus, Ohio
Circle-in the-Square Theater opens at 1633 Broadway NYC
MLB Chicago White Sox slugger Dick Allen wins AL MVP
Small Astronomy Satellite Explorer 48 launched to study gamma rays
Egypt & Israel exchange prisoners of war
International Energy Agency formed in Paris within OECD framework in the wake of the 1973 oil crisis
"Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys" single released by Ed Bruce
Syrian army conquers Beirut
183 die as Icelandic Airlines DC-8 crashes in Colombo, Sri Lanka
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates outfielder Dave Parker wins NL MVP
The documentary short "Heartbeat in the Brain", created by and starring Amanda Feilding, screens at the Suydam Gallery causing several viewers to faint
ABC-TV announces it would broadcast nightly specials on Iran hostage
Iran cancels all contracts with U.S. oil companies
Sir Anthony Blunt, art advisor to the Queen, is exposed in Parliament as the fourth man in a Soviet spy ring. He is then stripped of his knighthood and fellowship of Trinity College, Cambridge.
Big Thunder Mountain Railroad (a rollercoaster) opens at Disney World in Florida
Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus proclaimed
"Emergency" 16th studio album by American band Kool & the Gang is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1985)
A research assistant is injured when a package from the Unabomber addressed to a University of Michigan professor explodes
28 of 82 aboard Continental Airlines DC-9, die in crash at Denver
Carla Beurskens runs Dutch female record marathon (2:26:34)
Leile McBridge (Denver), crowned Miss Black America
NY Giant Raul Allegre kicks 2, 50 or more yard field goals in a game
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
91 m radio telescope dish at Green Bank, WV, collapses
Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Kirk Gibson wins NL MVP Award
Soviet space shuttle makes unmanned maiden flight (2 orbits)
The first Fairtrade label, Max Havelaar, is launched in the Netherlands.
"Batman" film is released on video tape
Bret Saberhagenwins AL Cy Young Award
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Walter Davis of the Denver Nuggets begins NBA free throw streak that will extend to 53 games
Producers confirm that Milli Vanilli didn't sing on their album
US 68th manned space mission STS 38 (Atlantis 7) launches into orbit
Dow Jones Industrial Average drops 120.31 points (5th largest dive)
Ricky Pierce (Seattle) begins NBA free throw streak of 75 game
Cuban Ilyushin IL-18 flight to Puerto Plata crashes, 34 die
Praveen Amre scores century on Test Cricket debut (103 v SA, Durban)
13 Cuban refugees land in Florida after stealing a crop-duster in Cuba
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Myrtle Beach, South Carolinsa on WYAV 104.1 FM
6.7-8.1 earthquake strikes Philippines, killing 45
Nepal Communist party Dutch Communist Party-UML wins election
Space shuttle Atlantis docks with orbiting Russian space station Mir
Next transit of Mercury visible in North America
A chartered Antonov AN-24 crashes after takeoff from Luanda, Angola killing more than 40 people
New state of Jharkhand comes into existence in India
Microsoft releases the first Xbox game console in the US
British drummerRingo Starrinducted into the Percussive Arts Society Hall Of Fame
First day of the Istanbul Bombings takes place, followed by additional bombings on November 20th
Boeing formally launches the stretched Boeing 747-8 variant with orders from Cargolux and Nippon Cargo Airlines
A devastating Cyclone named Sidr hits Bangladesh, killing an estimated 5000 people and destroying Sundarbans, the world's largest mangrove forest
Istana Negara, the new palace of the monarch of Malaysia with its 22 domes is inaugurated in Kuala Lumpur, replacing the old palace
At least 95 people are killed in Syrian conflicts
Deep Horizon Oil Spill: BP settles for $4.5 Billion
The Eurozone economy returns to recession with a fall of 0.1% in GDP in the third quarter of 2012 following a fall of 0.2% in the previous quarter
5 people are killed and 10 are injured after a train derails in Nashik, India
Sony launches the Playstation Four, selling one million units on the first day
A 7.3 magnitude earthquake strikes under the Molucca Sea in eastern Indonesia, triggering a tsunami warning
The parents of 43 Mexican students who disappeared start a nationwide bus tour in protest at the government's handling of the case
World leaders gather in Brisbane for G20 Summit, which will focus on economic growth
Christopher Columbusnotes in his journal the first recorded reference to tobacco
PopeClement VIItells England's King Henry VIII to end relationship with Anna Boleyn
Spanish expedition led byFrancisco Pizarrocaptures the Inca capital of Cusco after defeating an Inca army led by general Quisquis
Myles Standishleads 16 men in a foot exploration of the northern portion of Cape Cod
Prince William of Orange's army lands at Torbay, England; the 'Glorious Revolution' commences
PiratesAnne Bonny,Mary ReadandCalico Jackare captured by Capt. Jonathan Barnet and brought to the Spanish Town of Jamaica, for trial
José Miguel Carreratakes control of the Chilean government in a coup d’état becoming the country's first President
Felix Mendelssohn's "Symphony No. 5 (Reformation)" premieres
Charles Darwinreaches Tahiti on board HMS Beagle, the location where he would formulate his ideas later written in The Structure and Distribution of Coral Reefs
Union Major UnionGeneral Shermanleaves Atlanta on his "March to the Sea"
British naval officerDavid Beattypromoted to commander after Sudan Campaign
Morning Post reporterWinston Churchillcaptured by Boers in Natal
James J. JeffriesTKOs Gus Ruhlin in 6 for heavyweight boxing title in San Francisco
Leopold II, King of Belgium almost assassinated by Italian anarchist
King C. Gillettepatents the safety razor as the Gillette razor blade

American chocolatierMilton S. Hersheyand his wife Catherine donate a 486-acre piece of farmland, to establish the Hershey Industrial School for "poor, healthy, white male orphans" between  four and eight years old, providing them room board, and education until age 18
Italian socialistBenito Mussolinifounds newspaper Il Populo d'Italia

Canadian pilotWilliam George Barkerflying over Ancre River, spots concentration of German troops massing for counter-attack on Beaumont Hamel, sends emergency Zone Call to break up German infantry apart. Barker later receives Military Cross
British General Election: Conservative party wins a majority under AndrewBonar Law
FDRlays cornerstone of Jefferson Memorial in Washington, D.C.
Yugoslav government in exile names Draza Mihailovic premier
Chilean poetGabriela Mistralbecomes the first Latin American to be awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature
Ted Williamsis picked as AL MVP
Louis St. Laurentis sworn in as the 12th Prime Minister of Canada
Mackenzie Kingretires after 21 years and 154 days as Prime Minister of Canada
Dmitri Shostakovich' oratorio "Song of the Forests" premieres in Leningrad, USSR
"Love Me Tender" film withElvis Presleyin his acting debut premieres in the US
Richard "Dick" Hickock and Perry Smith murder four members of the Clutter Family at their farm outside Holcomb, Kansas (subject ofTruman Capotebook In Cold Blood)
Elgin Baylorof NBA LA Lakers scores 71 points vs NY Knicks
New York Yankees outfielderRoger Marisis voted AL MVP
Don Drysdalewins Cy Young Award
Mickey Wrightshoots a 62, the lowest golf score for a female professional at the Tall City Open in Midland, Texas
Craig Breedlovesets land speed record (600.601 mph-966.57 kph)
NASA Gemini 12 returns to Earth withJim LovellandBuzz Aldrin
Boston Red Sox outfielderCarl Yastrzemskiwins AL MVP
1st date in controversialJim Boutonbaseball diary "Ball Four"
Janis Joplin, accused of vulgar & indicent language in Tampa, Florida
Paul Brown, as head coach of the expansion Cincinnati Bengals, defeats his former team the Cleveland Browns, a moment he calls his "greatest victory"
Inteladvertises 4004-processor
Ringo Starrreleases singles "Goodnight Vienna" and "Only You" in the UK
US PresidentJimmy Carterwelcomes Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlavi
Harold Pinter's play "Betrayal" premieres in London
A package from the UnabomberTed Kaczynskibegins smoking in the cargo hold of a flight from Chicago to Washington, forcing the plane to make an emergency landing
PopeJohn Paul IIbegan 5 day visit to West Germany
Funeral service held in Moscow's Red Square for Soviet leaderLeonid Brezhnev
75th hat trick in NHL New York Islander franchise history, scored byMike Bossy
Gilberto Rodríguez Orejuelais captured in Spain while meeting to expand Cali Cartel business in Europe, but is later acquitted and returned to Colombia
2nd time "Saturday Night Live" uses a time delay (Sam Kinisonhosts)
Beastie Boysrelease their debut album "Licensed to Ill," the first #1 rap album on the Billboard charts
Brazilian Williams driverNelson Piquetretires from Australian Grand Prix at Adelaide with break trouble but wins his 3rd Formula 1 World Drivers Championship by 12 points fromNigel Mansell
Test Cricket debut of Waqar Younis &Sachin Tendulkarat Karachi
US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushsigns Clear Air Act of 1990
Joe Buttafuoco sentenced to 6 months for statutory rape ofAmy Fisher
German Bundestag officially electsHelmut Kohlas Chancellor by a single vote (341-340)
Hu Jintaobecomes the General Secretary of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China
Maria Sharapovabecomes the first Russian to win the tennis season-ending WTA Tour Championship; beats AmericanSerena Williams4–6, 6–2, 6–4 at the Staples Center, Los Angeles
Carrie Underwoodreleases her debut album "Some Hearts" (2006 Billboard Album of the Year, Grammy Award Best New Artist 2007)
"Take Care" second studio album byDrakeis released (Grammy Award Best Rap Album 2013)
Vladimir Putin's press secretary says media reports that the Russian president plans to leave the G20 Summit in Brisbane early are nonsense
Articles of Confederation, the first constitution of the United States, is approved by the Continental Congress
Isaac Pitmanintroduces his shorthand system of writing, Pitman shorthand
An estimated 2 million people take part in the Vietnam War Moratorium demonstration across the United States
Jean I, King of France and Navarre, (Nov 15-19, 1316), born in Paris (d. 1316 - he lived only 5 days)
Nicholas V [Tommaso Parentucelli], Italian Pope (1447-55), born in Sarzana, Republic of Genoa (d. 1455)
Eleanor of Austria, European noble - Queen of Portugal (1518-21), Queen of France (1530-47), Duchess of Touraine (1547–1558), born in Leuven, Habsburg Netherlands (now Belgium) (d. 1558)
Jacques Davy Duperron, French Roman Catholic cardinal, born in Saint-Lô, Normandy, France (d. 1618)
Albert VII, Archduke of Austria and cardinal-archbishop of Toledo, born in Wiener Neustadt, Austria (d. 1621)
Davis Mell, British violinist, composer, and clockmaker, born in Wilton, England (d. 1662)
Madeleine de Scudéry, French novelist (Artamène ou le grand Cyrus), born in Le Havre, Normandy, France (d. 1701)
Philips Koninck, Dutch painter, chiefly of landscapes (View at the mouth of a rive; The Feast of Bacchus), born in Amsterdam (d. 1688)
Nicolaus Adam Strungk, German composer, born in Braunschweig, Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg (d. 1700)
Hermann von der Hardt, German historian, born in Melle, Westphalia (d. 1746)
Christoph von Graffenried, Swiss nobleman and explorer who founded New Bern, British Province of Carolina (1710), born in Worb Castle, Bern, Switzerland (d. 1743)
Louis Bertrand Castel, French mathematician, born in Montpellier, France (d. 1757)
Eusebius Amort, German Roman Catholic theologian, born in Bibermuehle, Bavaria (d. 1775)
Gerhardus Havingha, Dutch organist and composer, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1753)
Louis-Gabriel Guillemain, French composer and violinist, born in Paris (d. 1770)
Johann Kaspar Lavater, Swiss writer, philosopher and founder of physiognomics, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1801)
Joseph Quesnel, French Canadian composer and playwright (Colas et Colinette), born in Saint-Malo, France (d. 1809)
Jan Blanken, Dutch hydraulic engineer (North Holland Canal), born in Bergambacht, Netherlands (d. 1838)
Heinrich Christian Friedrich Schumacher, Danish surgeon, botanist and professor of anatomy (research work in malacology), born in Glückstadt, Holstein, Germany (d. 1830)
William Horsley, English musician and composer, born in London (d. 1858)
Georges-Julien Sieber, French music publisher and composer, born in Paris (d. 1847)
Jérôme Bonaparte, King of Westphalia (1807-13), born in Ajaccio, Corsica (d. 1860)
Michel Chasles, French mathematician (geometry), born in Épernon, France (d. 1880)
George Rodwell, English composer, born in London (d. 1852)
Peter Hardeman Burnett, American politician, 1st Governor of California (1849-51), pushed to exclude African Americans from Oregon and California, born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1895)
Pleasant Adam Hackleman, American lawyer, politician and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Franklin County, Indiana (d. 1862)
John Banvard, American artist who painted worlds largest painting (3 mile canvas), born in New York City (d. 1892)
Joseph Bennett Plummer, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Barre, Massachusetts (d. 1862)
Petrus Abraham Samuel van Limburg Brouwer, Dutch novelist (Akbar), literary critic (De Gids), and politician (Member of Parliament, 1864-68), born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1873)[1]
Pierce M. B. Young, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Spartanburg, South Carolina (d. 1896)
Aleksey Apukhtin, Russian poet (I See You, My Dear, In Dreams), writer, and civil servant, born in Bolkhov, Russian Empire (d. 1893) [NS=11/27]
Christopher Hornsrud, Prime Minister of Norway (3 weeks), born in Skotselv, Øvre Eiker, Norway (d. 1960)
Adolf Bartels, German journalist and poet, born in Wesselburen, Holstein, Germany (d. 1945)
Ferruccio Giannini, Italian-American operatic tenor (one of the first to be recorded), teacher, and impresario (Verdi Opera House, Philadelphia), born in Barga, Italy (d. 1948)

Erich Tschermak von Seysenegg, Austrian botanist who was one of three scientists (also Hugo de Vries and Carl Correns) who independently rediscovered Gregor Mendel's work on the laws of genetics, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1962)
Robert de Flers, French author and playwright (Habit Vert), born in Pont-l'Évêque, France (d. 1927)

Birsa Munda, Indian tribal freedom fighter, religious leader and folk hero of the Munda tribe who led an uprising in Bengal against the British Raj, born in Ulihatu, Bengal Presidency, British India (d. 1900)
Lewis Stone, American actor (The Lost World, Prisoner of Zenda), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1953)
Franklin P. Adams, American columnist (Information Please), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1960)
Felix Frankfurter, American jurist and Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court (1939-62), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1965)
René Guénon, French-Egyptian metaphysician, traditionalist and author, born in Blois, France (d. 1951)
Hitoshi Ashida, Japanese politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1948), born in Fukuchiyama, Japan (d. 1959)
Marianne Moore, American poet (Pulitzer 1951, Collected Poems), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1972)
René Maran, French author (Batouala, 1st black writer to win Prix Goncourt), born in Martinique (d. 1960)
James Morrison, American actor (Wine of Youth, Little Detectives), born in Mattoon, Illinois (d. 1974)
Manuel II, last king of Portugal (1908-10), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1932)
Richmal Crompton, British author known for the "Just William" stories, born in Bury, England (d. 1969)
W. Averell Harriman, American politician (Gov-D-NY) and ambassador to USSR (1943-46), born in New York City (d. 1986)
Antoni Słonimski, Polish writer,  journalist and satirist, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1976)
Olga Nikolaevna, eldest daughter of the last Russian Tsar, Nicholas II and Tsarina Alexandra Feodorovna, born in Alexander Palace, Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1918)
Aneurin Bevan, Welsh-British politician (Member of Parliament, 1929-60; Minister of Health, 1945-51 - launched NHS), born in Tredegar, Monmouthshire, Wales (d. 1960)
Sacheverell Sitwell, English author (People's Palace), born in Scarborough, England (d. 1988)
Willy Alfredo [Willem Jue], Dutch entertainer and poet (Fish-Trap), born in Gouda, Netherlands (d. 1976)

Avdy Andresson, Estonian cavalryman (Czarist Cavalry, WWI; Estonian Army, 1918-40; Wehrmacht Cavalry, 1940-45), veterinarian, and Estonian Minister of War-In-Exile (1973-90), born in Viluvere, Livonia, Russian Empire (d. 1990)
Jack Ingram, American actor (Law of the West), and stuntman, born in Frankfort, Illinois (d. 1969)
Stewie Dempster, New Zealand cricket batsman (10 Tests, 2 x 100s; Wellington CA,	Leicestershire CCC, Warwickshire CCC), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1974)
Tilly Losch, Austrian dancer and actress (Duel in the Sun, Garden of Allah), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1975) [birth date disputed]
Annunzio Mantovani, Italian orchestra leader (Mantovani), born in Venice, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1980)
Curtis LeMay, American air force general (WWII) and VP candidate, born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1990)
Claus von Stauffenberg, German antifascist colonel (attempted to assassinate Hitler), born in Jettingen, Germany (d. 1944)
Arthur Haulot, Belgian journalist who spoke against the Nazi regime and was imprisoned at Dachau, born in Brussels, Belgium d. 2005)
Jorge Bolet, Cuban-born American pianist known for his performances of the work of Franz Liszt, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1990)
Bea Barrett, American golfer (Western Open 1938), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2002)
(José) "Bill" Meléndez, American Emmy Award-winning character animator (Peanuts TV specials), born in Hermosillo, Mexico (d. 2008)
Ruth Nita Barrow, 1st female Governor-General of Barbados (1990-95), born in Saint Lucy, Barbados (d. 1995)
Carol Bruce [Shirley Levy], American actress (Behind the Eight Ball; WKRP in Cincinnati - "Mother Carlson"), born in Great Neck, Long Island (d. 2007)
Joseph Wapner, American judge (People's Court), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2017)
Jerome Richardson, American jazz saxophonist, flute, clarinet, and piccolo player (Quincy Jones; Charles Mingus; Earl Hines), born in Oakland, California (d. 2000) [some sources cite birth on 12/25]
Wayne Thiebaud, American painter (Four Pinball Machines), born in Mesa, Arizona (d. 2021)
Francesco Rosi, Italian director (Lucky Luciano), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2015)
Giorgio Manganelli, Italian writer and journalist (Gruppo 63 movement), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1990)
Paul Acket, Dutch publisher (Music Express) and impresario (North Sea Jazz Festival), born in Semarang, Dutch East Indies (d. 1992)
Peter Hammond, British actor and director (The Buccaneers, King of the Castle), born in London, England (d. 2011)
Howard Baker, American politician (Senator-R-Tennessee 1967-85), Ronald Reagan's chief of staff, born in Huntsville, Tennessee (d. 2014)
Jurriaan Andriessen, Dutch pianist and composer (Berkshire Symphonies; Les Cloches des Clochards; Hedwig's Portrait), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1996)
Yuli Daniel, Russian writer and dissident convicted of anti-Soviet agitation, born in Moscow, Soviet Union (d. 1988)
Gregor Mackenzie, British Labour politician and Member of Parliament for Glasgow Rutherglen (1964-87), birthplace unknown (d. 1992)[1]
C. W. McCall [William Dale Fries, Jr.], American country singer ("Convoy"), born in Audubon, Iowa (d. 2022)
John Orchard, British actor (Capone, M*A*S*H), born in Kennington, London (d. 1995)
Seldon Powell, American jazz tenor saxophonist and flautist, born in Lawrenceville, Virginia (d. 1997)
William Heirens, serial killer (Lipstick Killer), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2012)
Ed Asner, American Emmy Award-winning actor (The Mary Tyler Moore Show - "Mr. Grant"; Lou Grant; Roots; Up), and activist, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2021)
J.G. Ballard, British novelist (Empire of the Sun; Crash), born in 1930 Shanghai International Settlement, Republic of China (d. 2009)
Olene Walker, politician (R), 1st female Governor of Utah (2003-2005), born in Ogden, Utah (d. 2015)
Robert Cohen, French boxer (NYSAC, NBA, The Ring bantamweight titles 1954-56), born in Bône, Algeria (d. 2022)
Whitman Mayo, American actor (Grady Wilson-"Sanford & Son"), born in New York City (d. 2001)
John Kerr, American actor (South Pacific, Peyton Place, Pit & Pendulum), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Mwai Kibaki, Kenyan politician, President of Kenya (2002-13), born in Gatuyaini, Kenya (d. 2022)
Pascal Lissouba, Congo politician, 1st democratically-elected President of the Republic of the Congo (1992-97), born in Tsinguidi, French Equatorial Africa (d. 2020)
Alvin Plantinga, American philosopher and Templeton Prize laureate (2017), born in Ann Arbor, Michigan

Clyde McPhatter, American R&B and doo-wop tenor vocalist, arranger and songwriter (The Dominos, 1950-53 - "Sixty Minute Man"; The Drifters, 1953-55 - "Honey Love"; solo - "Treasure My Love"), born in Durham, North Carolina (d. 1972)
Petula Clark, British pop singer-songwriter (Downtown; My Love; Don't Sleep in the Subway, and actress (Goodbye, Mr. Chips), born in Epsom, England
(Russell) "Rusty" Richards, American cowboy singer, yodeler, songwriter (Sons of the Pioneers, 1963-84), actor and film stuntman, born in Long Beach, California (d. 2023)
Barbara Cason, American actress (Comedy Tonight; Carter Country), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1990)
Jack Burns, American comedian (Burns & Schreiber), actor, and screenwriter (The Muppet Show), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2020)
(Charles) Felton Jarvis, American record producer (Elvis Presley, 1966-77; Charley Pride; Willie Nelson), and singer, born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1981)
Joanna Barnes, American actress (The Parent Trap; Spartacus; Goodbye Charlie), novelist, and newspaper columnist, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2022)

Peter Dickinson, British composer (Meditation on Murder in the Cathedral), pianist, and musicologist (CageTalk: Dialogues with & about John Cage), born in Lytham St Annes, Lancashire, England (d. 2023)
Ariston Muguranayanga Chambati, Zimbabwean politician and businessman, Minister of Finance, born in Chinhoyi, Rhodesia (1995)
Jerry Douglas, American Grabby Awards Hall of Fame film director, screenwriter, and playwright, born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 2021)
Pete Welding, American music writer (Down Beat), musicologist (Testament Records), and jazz and blues record producer (Doug Quattlebaum), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Wolf Biermann, German singer-songwriter and East German dissident, born in Hamburg, Germany
Jimmy Ellis, American soul singer (The Trammps - "Disco Inferno"), born in Rock Hill, South Carolina (d. 2012)
William "Little Willie" John, American R&B singer ("Fever"; "Leave My Kitten Alone"), born in Cullendale, Arkansas (d. 1968)
Enzo Staiola, Italian actor (Bruno Ricci in "Bicycle Thieves"), born in Rome, Italy (d. 2025)[1]
Erik Hansen, Danish canoeist (Olympic gold K-1 1000m 1960; World C'ship gold 1963), born in Randers, Denmark (d. 2014)
Thalmus Rasulala [Jack Crowder], American actor (Blacula; Roots), born in Miami, Florida (d. 991)
Yaphet Kotto, African-American stage and screen actor (Live And Let Die; Alien; Homicide: Life on the Street), born in Harlem, New York (d. 2021)[1]
Roberto Cavalli, Italian luxury fashion designer and inventor, born in Florence, Italy (d. 2024)
Sam Waterston, American actor (Capricorn One;, Heaven's Gate; I'll Fly Away; Law & Order), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts
Ulf Pilgaard, Danish actor known for "Farligt venskab", born in Skive, Denmark (d. 2024)
Whit Tucker, Canadian Football HOF wide receiver (Grey Cup 1968, 69 Ottawa Rough Riders; CFL All-Star	1967), born in Windsor, Ontario (d. 2024)
